subject: Compliance Audits
title: Part 5 - NIST CSF and DPDPA
description: Structured notes connecting the NIST Cybersecurity Framework with India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023. The phase explains cybersecurity-risk governance, digital personal data obligations, roles, rights, safeguards, breach response, and framework mapping.

topics.title: Master Relationship
topics.definition: DPDPA creates legal obligations around digital personal data, while NIST CSF provides a cybersecurity-risk structure that can help operationalize reasonable security safeguards.
keyPoints: DPDPA protects digital personal data.
DPDPA grants rights to Data Principals.
DPDPA requires reasonable security safeguards.
NIST CSF organizes cybersecurity risk.
NIST CSF defines desired security outcomes.
NIST CSF supports incident response and recovery.
Using NIST CSF alone does not automatically prove DPDPA compliance.
workflows: DPDPA legal obligations -> Reasonable security safeguards -> NIST CSF governance and risk outcomes -> Security implementation -> Evidence and improvement
oneLiners: DPDPA says what legal duties exist; NIST CSF helps structure how cybersecurity risk is managed.

topics.title: What Is NIST CSF
topics.definition: The NIST Cybersecurity Framework is voluntary guidance developed by the United States National Institute of Standards and Technology to help organizations manage cybersecurity risk.
keyPoints: It helps organizations understand cybersecurity risk.
It helps assess current cybersecurity practices.
It helps prioritize improvements.
It helps communicate risk.
It helps coordinate security activities.
It helps align cybersecurity with business objectives.
The framework is voluntary, risk-based, technology-neutral, sector-neutral, scalable, and outcome-oriented.
NIST CSF 2.0 applies to industry, government agencies, and other organizations regardless of size, sector, or maturity.
oneLiners: NIST CSF is a common language for cybersecurity risk outcomes.

topics.title: What NIST CSF Is Not
topics.definition: NIST CSF is guidance, not a law, certification, product list, or technical configuration checklist.
keyPoints: It is not a cybersecurity law.
It is not a certification.
It is not a mandatory product list.
It is not a replacement for risk assessment.
It does not guarantee that no breach will occur.
It defines desired outcomes but usually does not prescribe the exact technology to achieve them.
comparisons: Outcome: access to systems is controlled.
Possible implementations: MFA, RBAC, PAM, conditional access, and network segmentation.
oneLiners: NIST CSF defines outcomes, not a fixed shopping list.

topics.title: Three Main Components
topics.definition: The three major NIST CSF components are Framework Core, Profiles, and Implementation Tiers.
keyPoints: Framework Core asks what cybersecurity outcomes should be considered.
Profile asks where the organization is now and where it wants to be.
Tier describes how mature and integrated the cybersecurity-risk approach is.
oneLiners: Core defines outcomes; Profiles show gaps; Tiers describe risk-management maturity.

topics.title: Framework Core
topics.definition: The Framework Core organizes cybersecurity activities and outcomes into functions, categories, subcategories, and informative references.
keyPoints: Functions are the broadest security outcomes.
Categories group related cybersecurity outcomes.
Subcategories describe more specific results or activities.
Informative references map outcomes to standards and controls.
References may include ISO/IEC 27001, COBIT, CIS Controls, NIST SP 800-53, and ISA/IEC standards.
workflows: Functions -> Categories -> Subcategories -> Informative References
oneLiners: The Core turns cybersecurity into structured outcomes.

topics.title: CSF 1.1 vs CSF 2.0
topics.definition: CSF 1.1 used five functions, while CSF 2.0 uses six by adding Govern.
keyPoints: CSF 1.1 functions are Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover.
CSF 2.0 functions are Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover.
Govern emphasizes enterprise governance, policy, roles, legal obligations, risk strategy, supply-chain risk, and oversight.
Govern influences all other functions.
workflows: CSF 1.1: Identify -> Protect -> Detect -> Respond -> Recover
CSF 2.0: Govern -> Identify -> Protect -> Detect -> Respond -> Recover
oneLiners: The major CSF 2.0 update is Govern.

topics.title: Govern Function
topics.definition: Govern establishes how cybersecurity risk is directed, supervised, aligned with enterprise risk, and made accountable.
keyPoints: It includes organizational context.
It includes risk-management strategy.
It includes cybersecurity policy.
It includes roles and responsibilities.
It includes oversight.
It includes legal and regulatory requirements.
It includes supply-chain risk management.
It includes risk appetite and executive accountability.
workflows: Govern -> Identify + Protect + Detect + Respond + Recover
oneLiners: Govern directs and supervises cybersecurity risk.

topics.title: Identify Function
topics.definition: Identify develops an understanding of assets, business context, and cybersecurity risk.
keyPoints: It includes asset inventory.
It includes software inventory.
It includes data inventory and data flows.
It includes business environment and governance context.
It includes risk assessment and supply-chain risk.
It includes roles, responsibilities, and criticality classification.
oneLiners: Identify asks what exists, what matters, and what could go wrong.

topics.title: Protect Function
topics.definition: Protect implements safeguards to reduce the likelihood or impact of cybersecurity events.
keyPoints: It includes identity management.
It includes authentication and access control.
It includes awareness and training.
It includes data security.
It includes platform security, maintenance, and technology resilience.
Controls may include MFA, encryption, least privilege, backup, patching, secure configuration, and awareness training.
oneLiners: Protect asks which safeguards reduce exposure.

topics.title: Detect Function
topics.definition: Detect identifies cybersecurity anomalies, events, and warning signs.
keyPoints: It includes continuous monitoring.
It includes log analysis.
It includes security-event detection.
It includes anomaly detection and detection-process maintenance.
Controls may include SIEM, IDS, EDR, audit-log monitoring, alert correlation, and behavioral analytics.
oneLiners: Detect asks how the organization knows something suspicious is happening.

topics.title: Respond Function
topics.definition: Respond contains and manages detected cybersecurity incidents.
keyPoints: It includes incident management.
It includes analysis, communication, reporting, mitigation, coordination, and lessons learned.
Activities may include isolating affected systems, notifying the response team, preserving evidence, containing attacks, reporting breaches, and communicating with stakeholders.
oneLiners: Respond asks what happens after detection.

topics.title: Recover Function
topics.definition: Recover restores capabilities and improves resilience after an incident.
keyPoints: It includes recovery planning.
It includes restoration and communication.
It includes improvement and business continuity.
Activities may include restoring from backup, validating systems, rebuilding compromised endpoints, reviewing the incident, and updating controls.
oneLiners: Recover asks how operations are restored safely and improved afterward.

topics.title: Function Memory Line
topics.definition: The six NIST CSF 2.0 functions can be remembered through one action word each.
keyPoints: Govern means Direct.
Identify means Understand.
Protect means Defend.
Detect means Discover.
Respond means Contain.
Recover means Restore.
workflows: Govern -> Identify -> Protect -> Detect -> Respond -> Recover
Direct -> Understand -> Defend -> Discover -> Contain -> Restore
oneLiners: Direct, understand, defend, discover, contain, restore.

topics.title: Profiles
topics.definition: A Profile represents selected cybersecurity outcomes based on business needs, risk assessment, risk appetite, available resources, legal requirements, and mission priorities.
keyPoints: A Current Profile describes what the organization currently achieves.
A Target Profile describes what the organization intends to achieve.
Gap analysis compares current and target outcomes.
Gaps lead to a prioritized improvement plan.
workflows: Current Profile -> Compare with Target Profile -> Security Gaps -> Prioritized Improvement Plan
oneLiners: A Profile is a picture of current or desired cybersecurity outcomes.

topics.title: Implementation Tiers
topics.definition: Implementation Tiers describe how cybersecurity-risk practices are integrated into the organization.
keyPoints: Tier 1 is Partial.
Tier 2 is Risk Informed.
Tier 3 is Repeatable.
Tier 4 is Adaptive.
Tiers are qualitative descriptions of risk-management rigor and integration.
Tier 4 is not automatically pass, and Tier 1 is not automatically fail.
workflows: Tier 1 Partial -> Tier 2 Risk Informed -> Tier 3 Repeatable -> Tier 4 Adaptive
oneLiners: Tiers describe how deeply risk management is embedded.

topics.title: Tier 1 - Partial
topics.definition: Tier 1 describes an ad hoc, reactive, and inconsistent cybersecurity-risk approach.
keyPoints: Practices are reactive.
Awareness is limited.
Implementation is inconsistent.
Formal governance is minimal.
oneLiners: Tier 1 means cybersecurity risk is handled in a limited and inconsistent way.

topics.title: Tier 2 - Risk Informed
topics.definition: Tier 2 describes an organization where management is aware of risk and some practices exist, but implementation is not fully consistent or enterprise-wide.
keyPoints: Management is aware of cybersecurity risk.
Some risk-management practices exist.
Implementation may be inconsistent.
Risk management may not be fully enterprise-wide.
oneLiners: Tier 2 knows the risk but has uneven execution.

topics.title: Tier 3 - Repeatable
topics.definition: Tier 3 describes formal, consistent, and repeatable cybersecurity-risk practices.
keyPoints: Formal policies and processes exist.
Implementation is consistent.
Roles are defined.
Risk management is integrated.
Practices are reviewed.
oneLiners: Tier 3 means risk practices are defined and consistently repeated.

topics.title: Tier 4 - Adaptive
topics.definition: Tier 4 describes continuous improvement and adaptive cybersecurity-risk management.
keyPoints: Threat intelligence influences decisions.
Lessons learned are rapidly applied.
Processes adapt to changing threats.
Risk management is embedded in culture.
The organization improves continuously.
oneLiners: Tier 4 means cybersecurity risk management adapts as threats change.

topics.title: Profile vs Tier
topics.definition: Profiles describe cybersecurity outcomes, while Tiers describe the rigor and integration of risk-management practices.
keyPoints: Profile describes current and target outcomes.
Tier describes risk-management characteristics.
Profile supports gap analysis.
Tier supports understanding organizational approach.
Profile is outcome-based.
Tier is practice-integration based.
oneLiners: Profile says what outcomes are desired; Tier says how mature the risk approach is.

topics.title: NIST CSF Attributes
topics.definition: NIST CSF is designed as accessible, adaptable, risk-based, and useful for communication across many stakeholders.
keyPoints: It is written in accessible language.
It is adaptable across technologies.
It applies across lifecycle phases.
It is risk-based.
It links to international standards.
It is continuously updated.
It is influenced by public and private stakeholders.
It supports communication among executives, technical teams, auditors, regulators, vendors, risk teams, and business owners.
oneLiners: NIST CSF is a bridge language between business and cybersecurity.

topics.title: NIST CSF and Other Frameworks
topics.definition: NIST CSF can map desired outcomes to controls and practices from other frameworks and standards.
keyPoints: It can map to ISO/IEC 27001.
It can map to COBIT.
It can map to CIS Controls.
It can map to NIST SP 800-53.
It can support PCI DSS, HIPAA controls, GDPR security obligations, and DPDPA safeguards.
A NIST outcome such as asset inventory may be supported by ISO asset-management controls, CIS hardware inventory, COBIT processes, or NIST SP 800-53 controls.
workflows: NIST CSF Outcome -> Relevant Standards and Controls -> Implementation Evidence
oneLiners: NIST CSF can organize outcomes while other standards provide detailed controls.

topics.title: What Is DPDPA
topics.definition: DPDPA stands for the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, India's central law governing the processing of digital personal data.
keyPoints: It recognizes the individual's right to protect personal data.
It also recognizes the need to process personal data for lawful purposes.
The Act received assent on 11 August 2023.
It covers personal-data processing, consent, Data Principal rights, Data Fiduciary duties, Data Protection Board functions, and penalties.
oneLiners: DPDPA governs digital personal data in India.

topics.title: DPDPA Legal Status
topics.definition: The DPDPA framework consists of the Act, related rules, notifications, and phased implementation details.
keyPoints: The central statute is the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023.
Operational details may be provided through rules and government notifications.
Implementation may be phased.
For study, distinguish the Act, rules, notifications, and commencement timeline.
workflows: DPDP Act, 2023 -> Rules -> Notifications -> Phased Commencement
oneLiners: The Act creates the framework; rules and notifications operationalize it.

topics.title: Scope of DPDPA
topics.definition: DPDPA applies to processing of digital personal data within India and certain processing outside India connected to offering goods or services to individuals in India.
keyPoints: It covers data collected digitally.
It covers offline data that is later digitized.
It may apply outside India when processing is connected to offering goods or services to individuals in India.
It is not limited only to organizations physically located in India.
workflows: Digital Collection -> DPDPA Scope
Offline Collection -> Later Digitized -> DPDPA Scope
Outside India -> Goods or Services to Individuals in India -> DPDPA Scope
oneLiners: DPDPA follows digital personal data connected to India.

topics.title: Personal Data
topics.definition: Personal data is data about an identifiable individual. DPDPA focuses on personal data in digital form.
keyPoints: Personal data may include name, phone number, email address, customer ID, account information, IP address linked to a person, location data, online identifiers, employment data, health data, and financial data.
Digital personal data is personal data in digital form.
The key test is whether the data relates to an identifiable individual.
oneLiners: Personal data identifies or relates to a person.

topics.title: Key DPDPA Roles
topics.definition: DPDPA uses roles such as Data Principal, Data Fiduciary, Data Processor, and Significant Data Fiduciary.
keyPoints: Data Principal is the individual to whom personal data relates.
Data Fiduciary determines the purpose and means of processing personal data.
Data Processor processes personal data on behalf of a Data Fiduciary.
Significant Data Fiduciary is a high-impact fiduciary with additional obligations.
Data Principal is conceptually similar to GDPR's data subject.
Data Fiduciary is similar to GDPR's controller.
comparisons: Data Fiduciary = decides purpose and means.
Data Processor = processes on behalf of fiduciary.
oneLiners: Principal owns the relationship; Fiduciary decides; Processor performs.

topics.title: Significant Data Fiduciary
topics.definition: A Data Fiduciary may be classified as significant based on scale, sensitivity, risk, and public impact.
keyPoints: Factors may include volume of data, sensitivity, risk to Data Principals, impact on sovereignty, electoral democracy, security of the state, and public order.
Additional obligations may include Data Protection Officer, independent data auditor, Data Protection Impact Assessment, periodic audits, and higher governance requirements.
oneLiners: Higher data impact can create higher fiduciary obligations.

topics.title: Consent
topics.definition: Consent is a central processing ground under DPDPA and must be meaningful, clear, and withdrawable.
keyPoints: Valid consent should be free.
It should be specific.
It should be informed.
It should be unconditional.
It should be unambiguous.
It should be given through clear affirmative action.
It should be capable of being withdrawn.
Withdrawing consent should generally be as easy as giving it.
oneLiners: Consent must be clear, informed, and easy to withdraw.

topics.title: Notice
topics.definition: Notice explains personal-data processing before or at the time consent is requested.
keyPoints: Notice should explain what personal data is collected.
It should explain why data is collected.
It should explain how data will be used.
It should explain how rights may be exercised.
It should explain how consent can be withdrawn.
It should explain how grievances can be raised.
Consent without meaningful notice is not properly informed.
oneLiners: Notice makes consent informed.

topics.title: Legitimate Uses
topics.definition: DPDPA does not rely only on consent; certain specified legitimate uses may permit processing without ordinary consent depending on the situation.
keyPoints: Possible contexts include legal obligations.
They may include medical emergencies.
They may include employment-related purposes.
They may include state functions.
They may include voluntarily provided data in specified circumstances.
The statement that all DPDPA processing always requires consent is not fully correct.
oneLiners: Consent is central, but not the only processing route.

topics.title: Data Fiduciary Obligations
topics.definition: A Data Fiduciary must process data lawfully, protect it, manage consent and rights, supervise processors, and maintain accountability.
keyPoints: Process data lawfully.
Provide appropriate notice.
Obtain valid consent where required.
Maintain data accuracy where relevant.
Use reasonable security safeguards.
Prevent personal-data breaches.
Notify breaches as required.
Delete data when no longer required unless retention is legally justified.
Provide grievance redressal.
Supervise processors and honor applicable rights.
oneLiners: The fiduciary carries the main operational accountability.

topics.title: Reasonable Security Safeguards
topics.definition: DPDPA requires reasonable safeguards to prevent personal-data breaches.
keyPoints: Safeguards may include access control.
They may include MFA.
They may include encryption.
They may include logging and backup.
They may include vulnerability management and patch management.
They may include network security, secure development, vendor assessment, incident response, and employee training.
Frameworks such as NIST CSF and ISO 27001 help operationalize safeguards.
oneLiners: Reasonable safeguards turn legal duty into security controls.

topics.title: Personal-Data Breach
topics.definition: A personal-data breach may affect confidentiality, integrity, or availability of personal data.
keyPoints: It may involve unauthorized access.
It may involve disclosure.
It may involve loss.
It may involve alteration or destruction.
It may involve accidental sharing.
It may compromise confidentiality, integrity, or availability.
Organizations should report breaches according to the Act, Rules, and prescribed notification process.
oneLiners: A personal-data breach is not only leakage; it can also involve loss, alteration, or destruction.

topics.title: Rights of Data Principals
topics.definition: Data Principals have rights that allow them to understand, correct, update, erase, and challenge how their personal data is handled.
keyPoints: Rights include access or information.
Rights include correction.
Rights include completion.
Rights include updating.
Rights include erasure.
Rights include grievance redressal.
Rights include nomination of another person.
workflows: Access -> Correction -> Completion -> Updating -> Erasure -> Grievance Redressal -> Nomination
oneLiners: Data Principal rights make personal-data processing accountable.

topics.title: Right to Access Information
topics.definition: A Data Principal may seek information about personal data processing and related sharing or grievance mechanisms.
keyPoints: The individual may seek information about personal data being processed.
They may seek information about processing activities.
They may seek information about entities with whom data has been shared.
They may seek available grievance mechanisms.
oneLiners: Access rights create visibility into processing.

topics.title: Correction, Erasure and Grievance
topics.definition: Data Principals may seek correction, completion, updating, erasure, and grievance redressal through accessible mechanisms.
keyPoints: Individuals may request correction of inaccurate data.
They may request completion of incomplete data.
They may request updating of outdated data.
They may request erasure where retention is no longer justified.
Erasure is not absolute when another law requires retention.
A grievance process should include contact point, complaint submission, tracking, response timeline, escalation, documentation, and resolution evidence.
workflows: Deletion Request -> Check Legal Retention -> Erase if Permitted or Retain if Required -> Communicate Outcome
oneLiners: Erasure rights must be balanced with lawful retention duties.

topics.title: Nomination and Duties of Data Principals
topics.definition: DPDPA includes both rights and duties for Data Principals.
keyPoints: A Data Principal may nominate another person to exercise rights in the event of death or incapacity.
Data Principals should not impersonate another person.
They should not suppress material information.
They should not submit false particulars.
They should not file false or frivolous grievances.
They should not misuse statutory rights.
oneLiners: DPDPA gives individuals rights and also expects responsible conduct.

topics.title: Children's Data
topics.definition: Children's personal data requires special handling and protection.
keyPoints: Controls may include verifiable parental consent.
They may include restrictions on harmful processing.
They may include restrictions on tracking.
They may include restrictions on behavioral monitoring.
They may include restrictions on targeted advertising.
Special handling procedures may be needed.
Certain exceptions may be notified for specific classes or purposes.
oneLiners: Children's data requires stricter care and special controls.

topics.title: Cross-Border Transfers
topics.definition: DPDPA treats cross-border transfers as a compliance area, with restrictions possible through government notification and other Indian laws.
keyPoints: The broad DPDPA approach may allow transfers except to countries or territories restricted by government notification.
Other Indian laws may impose additional conditions.
GDPR relies on adequacy and transfer safeguards.
DPDPA relies on government-notified restrictions.
comparisons: GDPR: transfer mechanisms and adequacy safeguards.
DPDPA: government may restrict transfers to notified jurisdictions.
oneLiners: Cross-border transfer rules differ between GDPR and DPDPA.

topics.title: Data Protection Board of India
topics.definition: The Data Protection Board handles specified enforcement, inquiry, direction, complaint, breach, and penalty functions under DPDPA.
keyPoints: It may handle breach-related proceedings.
It may handle complaints.
It may conduct inquiries.
It may issue directions.
It may enforce compliance.
It may impose monetary penalties according to the legal framework.
oneLiners: The Board is the enforcement body for specified DPDPA matters.

topics.title: Penalties
topics.definition: DPDPA provides monetary penalties for failures based on the type and seriousness of the violation.
keyPoints: Failures may include inadequate security safeguards.
They may include failure to notify a breach.
They may include failure to meet children's-data obligations.
They may include failure by Significant Data Fiduciaries.
They may include repeated noncompliance.
oneLiners: Penalty exposure depends on the failure and its seriousness.

topics.title: DPDPA Compliance Workflow
topics.definition: DPDPA compliance can be organized as a workflow from personal-data discovery to ongoing improvement.
keyPoints: Identify personal data.
Map processing activities.
Classify the Data Fiduciary role.
Provide notice.
Identify lawful ground.
Obtain and manage consent where required.
Apply security safeguards.
Enable Data Principal rights.
Manage processors.
Prepare incident response.
Notify breaches as required.
Audit and improve.
workflows: Identify Personal Data -> Map Processing -> Classify Role -> Provide Notice -> Identify Lawful Ground -> Manage Consent -> Apply Safeguards -> Enable Rights -> Manage Processors -> Incident Response -> Breach Notification -> Audit and Improve
oneLiners: DPDPA compliance is data mapping, lawful processing, safeguards, rights, and response.

topics.title: Mapping DPDPA to NIST CSF
topics.definition: DPDPA obligations can be mapped to NIST CSF functions to structure security governance and safeguards.
keyPoints: Govern supports governance and accountability.
Identify supports data inventory and risk assessment.
Protect supports reasonable security safeguards.
Detect supports breach detection.
Respond supports breach handling and notification.
Recover supports restoration and improvement.
workflows: DPDPA Governance and Accountability -> Govern
Data and Asset Inventory -> Identify
Reasonable Security Safeguards -> Protect
Breach Detection -> Detect
Breach Handling and Notification -> Respond
Restoration and Improvement -> Recover
oneLiners: NIST CSF can organize the security side of DPDPA compliance.

topics.title: DPDPA Requirement Mapped to NIST CSF
topics.definition: Protecting customer personal data from unauthorized access can be structured through all six NIST CSF 2.0 functions.
keyPoints: Govern defines privacy-security policy, assigns responsibilities, and sets risk appetite.
Identify inventories personal data, maps data flows, identifies processors, and assesses risk.
Protect applies MFA, encryption, least privilege, and training.
Detect monitors logs, alerts on unusual downloads, and detects exfiltration.
Respond investigates breach, contains affected accounts, and notifies stakeholders and the Board as required.
Recover restores systems, validates data integrity, and improves failed controls.
workflows: Govern -> Identify -> Protect -> Detect -> Respond -> Recover
oneLiners: A legal safeguard duty can be translated into NIST CSF outcomes.

topics.title: DPDPA vs GDPR
topics.definition: DPDPA and GDPR both regulate personal data, but they differ in jurisdiction, terminology, rights, duties, breach timing, and transfer structure.
keyPoints: DPDPA belongs to India; GDPR belongs to the EU and EEA context.
DPDPA uses Data Principal; GDPR uses Data Subject.
DPDPA uses Data Fiduciary; GDPR uses Controller.
Both use Data Processor.
DPDPA regulator structure centers on the Data Protection Board of India; GDPR uses supervisory authorities.
DPDPA has explicit duties of Data Principals; GDPR is not framed the same way.
GDPR has an explicit right to portability and objection structure; DPDPA is more limited in the same form.
GDPR commonly uses a 72-hour authority notification rule, while DPDPA breach timing follows the Indian framework and prescribed process.
oneLiners: DPDPA resembles GDPR in privacy goals but differs in terminology and rights structure.

topics.title: NIST CSF vs ISO 27001
topics.definition: NIST CSF is a cybersecurity-risk framework, while ISO 27001 is a certifiable information security management-system standard.
keyPoints: NIST CSF is outcome-oriented.
ISO 27001 defines management-system requirements.
NIST CSF uses Core, Profiles, and Tiers.
ISO 27001 uses clauses, risk treatment, and controls.
NIST does not certify organizations against CSF.
ISO 27001 can be certified by third parties.
They can be used together: NIST CSF defines desired cybersecurity outcomes, while ISO 27001 sustains them through a management system.
oneLiners: NIST CSF gives outcomes; ISO 27001 gives a certifiable management system.

topics.title: Updated Unified Compliance Map
topics.definition: The updated compliance architecture places COBIT in governance, ISO 27001 in security management, NIST CSF in cyber-risk modeling, and laws or standards in domain-specific requirements.
keyPoints: COBIT supports the governance layer.
ISO 27001 supports security management.
NIST CSF 2.0 supports cyber-risk outcome modeling.
SOX supports financial reporting.
HIPAA supports health data protection.
PCI DSS supports card data protection.
GDPR supports EU data privacy.
DPDPA supports Indian digital personal data protection.
workflows: Enterprise -> Governance Layer: COBIT -> Security Management: ISO 27001 + Cyber Risk Model: NIST CSF 2.0 -> Legal and Industry Requirements: SOX + HIPAA + PCI DSS + GDPR + DPDPA
oneLiners: Governance, security management, cyber-risk outcomes, and legal duties form one compliance architecture.

topics.title: High-Probability Traps
topics.definition: Common mistakes occur when NIST versions, CSF components, DPDPA roles, consent, rights, safeguards, and compliance proof are confused.
keyPoints: NIST CSF 2.0 includes Govern.
NIST CSF is not a law or certification.
Profile and Tier are not the same.
DPDPA applies to digital personal data and offline data that is later digitized.
Data Fiduciary is similar to controller, not processor.
Consent is not the only lawful processing route under DPDPA.
Data Principals have duties as well as rights.
Erasure is not absolute when legal retention applies.
NIST CSF can support DPDPA safeguards but does not itself establish legal compliance.
Govern establishes strategy, accountability, and oversight; it is not merely a technical function.
oneLiners: Do not confuse legal duty, framework guidance, role names, and proof of compliance.

topics.title: One-Line Definitions
topics.definition: Short definitions summarize the main NIST CSF and DPDPA terms.
keyPoints: NIST CSF: a voluntary, risk-based framework that helps organizations govern, understand, protect against, detect, respond to, and recover from cybersecurity risk.
Framework Core: a hierarchy of cybersecurity outcomes organized into functions, categories, and subcategories.
Profile: a representation of selected current or target cybersecurity outcomes based on business and risk needs.
Implementation Tier: a qualitative description of how rigorously cybersecurity risk management is integrated into an organization.
DPDPA: India's law governing lawful processing and protection of digital personal data.
Data Principal: the individual to whom personal data relates.
Data Fiduciary: the entity that determines purpose and means of processing personal data.
Data Processor: an entity processing data on behalf of a Data Fiduciary.
Significant Data Fiduciary: a designated high-impact Data Fiduciary with additional compliance obligations.
Data Protection Board: the Indian body responsible for specified enforcement, inquiry, and penalty functions under DPDPA.
oneLiners: Definitions anchor the relationship between cybersecurity outcomes and personal-data duties.

topics.title: Final Memory Sentence
topics.definition: DPDPA establishes legal rights and duties around digital personal data in India, while NIST CSF provides a structured cybersecurity-risk model that can help organizations implement and demonstrate reasonable security safeguards.
keyPoints: DPDPA creates legal accountability for digital personal data.
NIST CSF structures cybersecurity governance and risk outcomes.
The combined approach connects legal duties with practical safeguards.
The six CSF 2.0 functions are Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover.
workflows: DPDPA legal duty -> NIST CSF 2.0 functions -> Reasonable safeguards -> Monitoring and evidence -> Improvement
oneLiners: DPDPA defines the duty; NIST CSF structures the safeguard program.
